metadata.title: Yield Curve Bootstrapping — Zero Rates from Coupon Bonds with Claude
metadata.note: CLI video spine: B00 intro · B01 PROBLEM · B02 ASK · B03 CODE · B04 OUTPUT(manim) · B05 CHANGE · B06 slate · B07 slate · B08 slate · B09 outro
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)

then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: B00: palette=claude but the cold open is 'NikBearBrownOpen' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B09: palette=claude but the outro is 'NikBearBrownOutro' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

When you price a derivative, you need to know the zero-coupon yield for every maturity — the risk-free rate with no intermediate cash flows. But the market only trades coupon bonds. Bootstrapping extracts the zero rates from coupon bond prices, one maturity at a time, using each previously-solved zero rate to strip the coupon payments. Today we build the bootstrapper with Claude for 5 bonds spanning 1 to 10 years.
beats.visual_intent: title card: coupon bond cash flows stripped into zero-coupon spots

build.suggested: suggested prompt: When you price a derivative, you need to know the zero-coupon yield for every maturity — the risk-free rate with no intermediate cash flows. But the market only

beats.narration_text: Type the prompt. Bootstrap a zero-coupon yield curve from 5 coupon bonds — maturities 1, 2, 3, 5, and 10 years. Print the maturity, zero rate, discount factor, and implied par rate for each maturity.

props.command:
claude "Write yield_curve.py: bootstrap zero-coupon yield curve from
5 coupon bonds: [(100,0.03,1,99.5),(100,0.04,2,99.2),
(100,0.045,3,99.0),(100,0.05,5,98.5),(100,0.055,10,96.0)].
Print: Maturity | Zero Rate (%%) | Discount Factor | Implied Par Rate."

beats.narration_text: Read it. The 1-year zero rate comes directly from the 1-year bond price: z1 equals the total 1-year cash flow divided by price, minus one. The 2-year zero rate uses z1 to discount the year-1 coupon, leaving only the year-2 cash flow to pin z2. Each successive rate uses all previously solved rates. The discount factor is just exp of negative z times T for continuous compounding.

props.code:
# yield_curve.py  —  Claude Code output
zero_rates = {}

# 1-year bootstrap: direct from 1yr bond
price_1yr = 99.5
coupon_1yr = 100 * 0.03  # = 3.0
z1 = (face + coupon_1yr) / price_1yr - 1  # = 0.0352
zero_rates[1] = z1

# 2-year bootstrap: strip year-1 coupon using z1
def pv_coupon(c, t, z_dict):  # PV of coupon at t given zero rates
    return c * np.exp(-z_dict[t] * t)
# Solve for z2 from 2yr bond price...

beats.narration_text: Run it. The crimson dots are the bootstrapped zero rates — rising from 3.5 percent at 1 year to 6.3 percent at 10 years. The grey dots are the bond yields-to-maturity — similar but slightly different because they average across the coupon payments. The upward slope is called a normal yield curve: longer maturities demand higher rates as compensation for duration risk.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: bootstrapped zero rate curve (crimson) with dots, bond YTM dots (grey), upward slope labeled

beats.narration_text: What happens if the 5-year bond price drops — say, from 98.5 to 95? That would push the 5-year zero rate up, creating a kink in the curve. Ask Claude to rerun with the repriced bond and show the curve change.

props.command:
claude "update yield_curve.py:
  -> change 5yr bond price from 98.5 to 95.0
  -> rebootstrap and compare zero rates at years 3, 5, 10
  -> plot the shifted curve vs original"

beats.narration_text: Dropping the 5-year price to 95 pushes the 5-year zero rate from 5.4 to 7.2 percent. The 10-year rate also shifts because it bootstraps off z5. Each bond in the chain is a lever — reprice one, and every longer-dated rate moves. That is why yield curve construction is sensitive to the liquid benchmark bonds you choose.
beats.visual_intent: summary card: two zero curves overlaid — original vs repriced; kink at 5yr labeled

beats.narration_text: Your move: extend to a full term structure by adding cubic spline interpolation between the bootstrapped knot points. The Nelson-Siegel model is the standard parametric alternative — 4 parameters, smooth curve, fits the level, slope, and curvature of yields.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: cubic spline vs Nelson-Siegel; 4 parameters labeled

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Your move: extend to a full term structure by adding cubic spline interpolation between the bootstrapped knot points. The Nelson-Siegel model is the standard pa

beats.narration_text: Then use the zero curve to price a swap. A fixed-for-floating interest rate swap is just the difference of two bond prices: the fixed leg discounted at swap rates, the floating leg always close to par. The swap rate that makes the two equal is exactly what you read off the bootstrapped curve.
beats.visual_intent: code snippet: swap pricing from zero curve; fixed leg PV vs floating leg PV

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Then use the zero curve to price a swap. A fixed-for-floating interest rate swap is just the difference of two bond prices: the fixed leg discounted at swap rat
